---
title: "Tune compute resources"
description: "Nextflow labels for CPU, memory, and disk."
categories: [openpipeline]
order: 90
---

[`openpipeline`]{.pkg-tag .pkg-openpipeline}<span class="swatch openpipeline"></span>

Every OpenPipeline component declares its CPU and memory needs through a
**resource label**. Labels are defined once in
`src/workflows/utils/labels.config` and applied per component, so a workflow
inherits sensible defaults from each step it runs.

## The standard labels

| Label | CPUs | Memory (attempt 1) |
|---|---|---|
| `singlecpu` | 1 | — |
| `lowcpu` | 4 | — |
| `midcpu` | 10 | — |
| `highcpu` | 20 | — |
| `lowmem` | — | 4 GB |
| `midmem` | — | 25 GB |
| `highmem` | — | 50 GB |
| `veryhighmem` | — | 75 GB |

Labels combine — a component tagged `[highcpu, midmem]` requests 20 CPUs and
25 GB. Requests scale automatically on retry, so a step that runs out of memory
is resubmitted with more.

There are also disk labels (`lowdisk`, `middisk`, `highdisk`, `veryhighdisk`),
but unlike CPU and memory they have **no value by default** — components carry
the label without it requesting any disk. When processing very large datasets,
give them an effect by setting sizes in your compute environment, for example
`withLabel: highdisk { disk = { 200.GB * task.attempt } }`.

## Override resources at run time

To cap or raise resources for a specific run, supply a Nextflow config with a
`process` scope and select it with `-c`. Target a label with `withLabel:` or a
specific process with `withName:`.

```groovy
// my_resources.config
process {
  withLabel: highmem   { memory = 32.GB }
  withLabel: highcpu   { cpus   = 16 }
}
```

For finer control, target a single process by name with `withName:`. This
overrides any resource labels that process carries, so you can bump one
component without touching everything that shares its label:

```groovy
// my_resources.config
process {
  withName: '.*:scvi:.*' { memory = 64.GB; cpus = 24 }
}
```

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/multiomics/process_samples/main.nf \
  --param_list samples.csv \
  --publish_dir results/ \
  -c my_resources.config
```

:::{.callout-tip}
The same `-c config` mechanism is how you point a run at cloud executors
(AWS Batch, Google Cloud Batch, an HPC scheduler). See the
[Nextflow executor docs](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/executor.html).
:::

## GPU acceleration

Components that wrap natively GPU-capable tools — such as `cellbender`, `scvi`,
`scanvi`, `scarches`, and `celltypist` — already carry a **`gpu` label** in
addition to their CPU/memory labels. They run on CPU by default; to schedule
them onto a GPU, request GPU resources for the `gpu` label with the
[`accelerator`](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#accelerator)
directive in a config file:

```groovy
// gpu.config
process {
  withLabel: gpu {
    accelerator = 1
    // plus any executor- / runtime-specific GPU settings
  }
}
```

```bash
nextflow run ... -c gpu.config
```

The exact settings depend on your executor and container runtime — see the
[Nextflow accelerator / GPU documentation](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#accelerator).
GPU drop-ins for the steps whose tools have no native GPU path are covered in
[Speed up with GPUs](gpu.qmd).

## Set resources on your own component

When [creating a component](../contributing/creating-components.qmd), assign
labels in its Nextflow runner directives:

```yaml
runners:
  - type: nextflow
    directives:
      label: [highcpu, midmem]
```

Inside scripts, read `meta_cpus` and `meta_memory_gb` for resource-aware
parallelism rather than hardcoding thread counts.

**Next:** [Create a component →](../contributing/creating-components.qmd)
